stem: Suppose \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} h(x)\, dx = [[P]] \). Use the orientation rule \( \int_{p}^{q} h = -\int_{q}^{p} h \) together with linearity to evaluate \( \int_{[[b]]}^{[[a]]} \left( ([[m]])h(x) + ([[k]]) \right) dx \).
solution: Reversing the limits flips the sign: \( \int_{[[b]]}^{[[a]]} h = -\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} h = -([[P]]) \). The constant term integrates over the (also reversed) interval, contributing \( ([[k]])\left([[a]] - [[b]]\right) \). By linearity the total is \( ([[m]])\left(-([[P]])\right) + ([[k]])([[a]]-[[b]]) = [[answer]] \).

solution: Chaining interval additivity across all three adjacent pieces, \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[d]]} f = \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f + \int_{[[b]]}^{[[c]]} f + \int_{[[c]]}^{[[d]]} f = [[P]] + [[Q]] + [[R]] \). By linearity, \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[d]]} ([[m]])f = ([[m]])([[P]]+[[Q]]+[[R]]) = [[answer]] \).

stem: A factory's production rate is \( r(t) \) units per day. Records show \( \int_{[[t1]]}^{[[t2]]} r(t)\, dt = [[M1]] \) (total units made from day [[t1]] to day [[t2]]) and \( \int_{[[t2]]}^{[[t3]]} r(t)\, dt = [[M2]] \) (from day [[t2]] to day [[t3]]). If a scaled process runs at rate \( [[s]]\cdot r(t) \) instead, how many units would it produce from day [[t1]] to day [[t3]]?
solution: By interval additivity the total units at the original rate from day [[t1]] to day [[t3]] is \( \int_{[[t1]]}^{[[t2]]} r + \int_{[[t2]]}^{[[t3]]} r = [[M1]] + [[M2]] \). Running at \( [[s]]\cdot r(t) \) scales this total by linearity: \( [[s]]\cdot\left([[M1]]+[[M2]]\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only accounts for the first period and ignores the second.
This forgets to scale the total by the new rate multiplier \( [[s]] \).
This subtracts the second period's production instead of adding it.
This only accounts for the second period and ignores the first.
This is off by \( [[s]] \) from the correct value.

This is off by one from the correct value.
